name: Death and Mortality
shortDescription: Death was viewed in Sumerian mythology as an inevitable and often feared aspect of existence, a transition to the underworld, Kur. Mortality was a defining characteristic separating humans from the gods, fueling a desire for immortality.
description: In Sumerian mythology, death and mortality held a complex and often grim significance. Unlike the gods who possessed eternal life, humans were destined to die, a fate that marked a fundamental distinction between the divine and mortal realms. Death was not necessarily seen as an end, but rather as a transition to Kur, the Sumerian underworld, a dark and dusty realm ruled by Ereshkigal. Life in Kur was generally viewed as bleak and undesirable, a shadowy existence where the deceased were sustained by offerings from the living. The fear of death and the desire to overcome mortality are prominent themes in Sumerian literature, most notably in the Epic of Gilgamesh. Gilgamesh's quest for immortality reflects the human struggle to reconcile with the inevitability of death and to find meaning in a finite existence. The Sumerians believed that proper burial rites and continued remembrance by the living were crucial for the well-being of the deceased in Kur. Neglect of these duties could lead to the deceased becoming restless spirits, potentially causing harm to the living. While immortality was generally unattainable for humans, achieving lasting fame or leaving behind a lasting legacy was seen as a way to transcend the limitations of mortality, ensuring one's name and deeds would be remembered for generations to come. The contrast between the fleeting nature of human life and the eternal existence of the gods was a constant source of contemplation and a driving force behind many Sumerian myths and cultural practices.

sources.title: Ancient Near Eastern Texts Relating to the Old Testament
sources.author: James B. Pritchard
